fingerprint: Eclipse coverage percentages are computed from Besselian elements — globe-only astronomical infrastructure
globe_value: Besselian eclipse geometry: Earth modeled as WGS84 oblate spheroid (R ≈ 6,371 km), Moon at ~384,400 km, Sun at ~150M km. Shadow cone intersects spherical surface to compute coverage fractions per station. JPL DE440/441 ephemerides provide Moon/Sun positions in geocentric coordinates on a sphere.
dome_value: Coverage percentages used directly in dome formula: Ebro 0.95, San Pablo 0.90, Eskdalemuir 0.55, Lerwick 0.42, Hartland 0.80, Niemegk 0.40, Canary Islands 0.72, Chambon 0.70, Coimbra 0.92. No dome-derived eclipse geometry computation exists — these numbers come from standard astronomical ephemerides.
match_precision: Exact import — coverage percentages are not approximated or re-derived; they are standard Besselian element outputs used verbatim
why_it_matters: The dome's eclipse formula delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF has three parameters. The baseline (-10.9 nT) is the 2017 observation itself. The FSF values are hardcoded per-station constants. The coverage percentages are the only parameter with a physical computation behind them — and that computation is entirely globe-dependent. Besselian elements (Bessel 1824) project the Moon's shadow cone onto a 'fundamental plane' through the Earth's center, then compute the intersection of the projected shadow with a spherical (WGS84) Earth surface. This method requires: (1) a spherical/oblate Earth with known radius, (2) a distant Moon at ~384,400 km following Keplerian orbits, (3) geocentric coordinates for both luminaries. On the dome's flat disc with a local moon at 2,534 km altitude, the shadow geometry would be fundamentally different: the shadow cone from a nearby small moon would produce a different umbra/penumbra size, different path across the disc surface, and different coverage fractions at each station. The dome provides no alternative eclipse geometry computation using its own parameters (disc_radius=20,015 km, moon_altitude=2,534 km). It silently imports the globe model's shadow geometry as its coverage input. This is structural parasitism: the dome's only 'predictive' eclipse formula cannot function without globe-derived ephemerides. Remove the Besselian elements and the dome has no way to compute coverage — and therefore no way to make any eclipse prediction at all.
additional_globe_dependencies: Station positions (Ebro, Eskdalemuir, etc.) are identified by WGS84 latitude/longitude coordinates, which are spherical-Earth infrastructure. The dome maps these to disc radius r via the azimuthal equidistant projection — itself a globe-to-flat transformation.

The eclipse path of totality (crossing Spain on Aug 12, 2026) is computed from lunar orbital mechanics assuming a distant Moon in Keplerian orbit around a sphere. The dome's local moon at 2,534 km would trace a different path entirely.
INTERMAGNET station data (BOU magnetometer readings) uses WGS84 coordinates and geomagnetic field models (IGRF/CHAOS) referenced to a spherical harmonic expansion — globe-only mathematics.
suggested_text: The dome's eclipse formula (δZ = baseline × coverage × FSF) is structurally dependent on globe astronomy. The coverage percentages — 0.95 at Ebro, 0.42 at Lerwick, etc. — are computed from Besselian eclipse elements, a method that projects the Moon's shadow cone onto a spherical Earth surface using JPL ephemerides. On a flat disc with a local moon at 2,534 km, the shadow geometry would be entirely different: different umbra size, different path, different station coverage. The dome provides no alternative shadow computation using its own parameters. Without the globe's Besselian elements, the dome cannot compute a single coverage percentage — and therefore cannot make any quantitative eclipse prediction.
already_noted_in_review: Partially. The curmudgeon Cycle 2 review (WIN-010.c2.json) notes that coverage percentages use 'standard Besselian eclipse geometry (JPL ephemerides), not dome geometry' and the detail_evidence mentions this. But the review does not frame it as a globe fingerprint or emphasize the structural impossibility of computing eclipse coverage on a flat disc with a local moon. The Besselian method's fundamental dependence on a spherical Earth is not made explicit — it is treated as a background fact rather than a devastating structural argument.
